options: hottest ▲ 下篇短文共有 5 個空格,為第 26-30 題,請依短文文意,選出一個最適合該空格的答案。 Milk consumption is estimated at billions of liters worldwide. Most people drink cow’s milk to get protein and calcium. Goat milk, 26 to be more nutrient-dense than cow’s milk, is also commonly found on kitchen tables in many cultures in the world. Desert nomad tribes in Africa, the Middle East and Asia have depended 27 camels for milk for centuries. But have you ever wondered why humans don’t consume pig milk? Pigs are the primarily domesticated animals. We’ve got heaps of them. But why don’t people milk them? It turns out that it’s nearly impossible to milk pigs. When sows are producing milk, they get very aggressive. They are not 28 to control as cows, and they are not cooperative, either. They simply do not like you getting near them. You would even have trouble 29 a pig to milk it. Plus, pigs do not produce that much milk when compared to cows. 30, with high production costs and low yield, milking pigs is not worth the effort.

questions.subject: 英文
questions.unit: 110-5-四技二專-00共同科目 英文
questions.q: What is the most likely meaning of the underlined word “ masks ”?
options: exchanges one thing for another thing ▲ 閱讀下文,回答第 40-42 題 If you visit Kaunus, Lithuania, you may come across the name of Sugihara several times. In fact, the year 2020 was “The Year of Chiune Sugihara” in Lithuania. Why is the Japanese so famous in Lithuania? Sugihara was the first Japanese diplomat stationed in Lithuania. When World War II broke out in 1939, Chiune Sugihara was appointed as a vice-consul in Kaunus. His job was to carefully watch and gather information about Japan’s ally, Germany. Meanwhile, Nazi tanks had already begun to roll in neighboring Poland, causing Jewish refugees to flee into Lithuania. When the Soviet Union invaded Lithuania in June of 1940, plenty of Jews flooded into the Japanese consulate, seeking transit visas to be able to escape to a safe place through Japan. Although the foreign ministry in Tokyo did not permit Sugihara to issue visas, he decided to disobey government orders because he couldn’t leave the Jews in Lithuania to their fate. He began issuing transit visas for those in need. For six weeks in the summer of 1940, Sugihara issued and signed as many visas as he could, sometimes working 18 hours a day. When th the consulate was closed, he moved to a hotel in Kaunus center and continued issuing the visas. On the 4 of September that year, Sugihara left Kaunus by train to Germany. In official documents, 2,139 visas were issued, but experts estimate that 6,000 Jewish lives may have been saved by Sugihara because in many cases, an entire family traveled on a single visa. The visas issued by Sugihara would eventually be called “visas for life.”
